Caption: Side, top and front views, including the battery module, the structural elements and the instrumentation within the ISO 9705 room. The locations of the objects are referenced to the origin of coordinates, as indicated in the top view. All units are given in meters. PTC, TC and TC_Tree refer to the plate thermometers, single thermocouples and thermocouples distributed in trees, respectively.

Experimental set up and instrumentation within the ISO 9705 room. The illustration shows the position of the battery module upper three lightweight solid concrete blocks, structural concrete elements, pilot flame, plate thermocouple (PTCs), thermocouples (TCs), and Gopro cameras. PTC_WL, PTC_WH, PTC_J, PTC_C and PTC_M refer to the plate thermometers for the wall low, wall high, jet, ceiling and module, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1. The distribution of thermocouples is indicated for TC_TreeJet.

Top view figure: Configuration of the battery module: battery cells (1 to 28), plastic holders, plastic cover with ventilation opening, plastic endplate and metal casing. TC in the different cells are indicated with red dots. Side view figure: The distances A and B of TCs from the cell edges are indicated by a red dot in cell number 28 and in cell number 1. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The horizontal axis shows the cell number, while the vertical axis represents the time elapsed (min) since the occurrence of the first thermal runaway for all the experiments. The vertical axis at 0 indicates how many individual cells contributed to the initial thermal runaway. The figure is based in Meraner et al. [40].

Cell surface temperature vs time (min) within the battery modules during all the experiments. Thermocouples were placed within the battery module (TC_batt1 to TC_batt28) as described in section 2.3. Each image shows selected thermocouples located on cells at both the edges and the centre of the battery. Some TCs were not included because the measurements after thermal runaway were affected by noise.
Images of the fire development for Experiment 1.
Images of the jet flame directions from Experiment 1 captured by GoPro cameras: The observed jet flame directions include horizontal and diffusive flames with two inclination angles, 60 and 90°.
Images of the fire development for Experiment 2.

Gas temperatures measured in the three thermocouple trees a) TreeJet (at 230 cm, 130 cm and 80 cm), b) TreeMiddle (at 230 cm, 130 cm and 80 cm), and c) TreeBack (at 230 cm, 130 cm and 80 cm), ISO 834 fire curve and hydrocarbon fire curve are represented during experiment 1, 2, and 3. The temperature range for the three thermocouples trees, from 230 cm to 80 cm, is illustrated as a coloured grey area for all experiments.

Caption: Incident radiation (kW/m2) is represented using two plate thermometers in all experiments: a) Plate thermometer is positioned in front of jet flame (PTC_WL), and b) Plate thermometer is oriented toward the ceiling (PTC_C).
Gas measurements of CO2 and CO in the exhaust duct of the ISO 9705 setup. Results from Experiment 3 are not available due to a malfunction in the gas analyser.

For Experiment 1: (a) The heat release rate (kW) was measured over a 70-min period. (b) The heat release rate (kW) of the LIB module was specifically examined between 19 and 29 min to capture the detailed combustion behaviour during this timeframe.

For Experiment 2: (a) The heat release rate (kW) was recorded over a duration of 70 min. (b) The heat release rate (kW) of the LIB module was specifically analysed from 19 min to 26 min to capture detailed combustion dynamics during this period.

Representation of heat release rate (kW) by OCC and CDG methods, and the ultra-fast fire curve with growth constant of 1.25 min/√MW. a) Heat release rate from 19 min to 22 min for Experiment 1, b) Heat release rate from 20 min to 23 min for Experiment 2.
